sets.title: は (wa) vs が (ga)
sets.description: は marks the topic — what the sentence is about. が marks the grammatical subject — what performs the action or bears the property. The choice changes information structure, not core grammar.

pairs.question: Which sentence introduces the elephant as new, prominent information in the scene?
pairs.explanation: 「象は」(wa) establishes the elephant as the topic of a general statement — "As for elephants, (their) noses are long." The elephant is background. 「象が」(ga) presents the elephant as the focal new subject — the very thing that came. が highlights the noun as the specific answer to an implicit "who?" question.

pairs.question: Which sentence implies Tanaka was the one who came (others may not have)?
pairs.explanation: 「田中さんは来た」(wa) says: speaking of Tanaka, he came — it doesn't exclude others. 「田中さんが来た」(ga) says: it was Tanaka who came — が has an exhaustive or contrastive nuance that singles out Tanaka as the specific person. This distinction is invisible in most English translations.

sets.title: 〜ている — Ongoing Action vs Resultant State
sets.description: 〜ている has two main readings depending on the verb type: with activity verbs it marks ongoing action; with change-of-state verbs it marks the state that results from a completed change.

pairs.question: Which sentence describes a permanent (or long-lasting) condition rather than an activity in progress?
pairs.explanation: 「走っている」uses 走る (to run) — an activity verb. ている marks it as in progress: she is running right now. 「結婚している」uses 結婚する (to get married) — a change-of-state verb. ている marks the resulting state: she is married (the wedding happened, and the result persists). Same grammar, different aspect reading.
